data.description:

Approach 1: Equinor Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projections of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s value using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future cash flows are worth in present dollar terms.

For Equinor, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on free cash flow, or FCF. The latest twelve month FCF is reported at US$8,964.23m. Analyst estimates are provided out to 2029, such as US$4,184m in 2026 and US$5,782.50m in 2029, with further projections out to 2035 extrapolated by Simply Wall St rather than based on new analyst forecasts.

After discounting each of these projected cash flows back to today, the model produces an estimated intrinsic value of NKr594.35 per share. Against the current share price of NKr247.40, this implies an intrinsic discount of about 58.4%, which suggests that Equinor is priced well below this DCF estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Equinor Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Equinor, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings the business is generating today. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see the earnings as relatively resilient, and a lower P/E when they see more risk or less attractive growth.

Equinor currently trades on a P/E of 10.68x. That sits below the Oil and Gas industry average P/E of 13.82x and also below the peer group average of 14.72x. To go a step further, Simply Wall St calculates a “Fair Ratio” for Equinor of 10.60x. This is a proprietary estimate of what Equinor’s P/E might be given factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margin, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more useful than simple peer or industry comparisons because it adjusts for Equinor’s own characteristics rather than assuming one size fits all. Equinor’s actual P/E of 10.68x is very close to the Fair Ratio of 10.60x, which points to the shares being priced roughly in line with this metric.

For Harrow, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $75.15 million, so the valuation leans heavily on future projections rather than current cash generation.

Analysts and internal estimates project free cash flow rising to $416.40 million in 2030, with intermediate projections between 2026 and 2035 ranging from $81.70 million to $660.05 million. Simply Wall St uses analyst estimates where available and then extrapolates the later years to complete the 10 year cash flow path.

Discounting these projected cash flows back to today results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $324.20 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $48.49, the model implies Harrow trades at roughly an 85.0% discount. This points to the stock screening as significantly undervalued on this measure.

Approach 2: Harrow Price vs Sales

For companies that are not yet consistently profitable, the P/S ratio is often a useful yardstick because it compares what investors are paying to the revenue the business is already generating, without relying on earnings that can be negative or volatile.

Growth expectations and risk still matter because a higher growth outlook or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” P/S multiple, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually supports a lower one. Context is therefore important when interpreting any single number.

Harrow currently trades on a P/S of 7.18x, compared with the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 4.81x and a peer group average of 8.22x. data.title: Is Uniti Group (UNIT) Fairly Priced After Recent Telecom Infrastructure Coverage?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Uniti Group’s share price reflects its true worth, you are not alone. This article focuses on what the current price might be telling you about value.
  • The stock recently closed at US$7.50, with returns of 2.7% over 7 days, 1.8% over 30 days, 9.8% year to date, a 22.7% decline over 1 year and a 51.0% decline over 5 years.
  • Recent news coverage around Uniti Group has highlighted its position as a telecommunications infrastructure owner, with commentary often centering on balance sheet resilience and how investors view its asset base. This backdrop helps frame why the share price has seen shorter term gains alongside longer term weakness.
  • On Simply Wall St’s 6 point valuation checklist, Uniti Group scores 3 out of 6. We will unpack this using different valuation methods, before finishing with a broader way to think about what valuation really means for you as a shareholder.

Approach 1: Uniti Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Uniti Group is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Uniti Group, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $146.97 million. Analyst inputs and Simply Wall St extrapolations then project annual free cash flow out to 2035, shifting from negative figures in the nearer years to a projected $205.04 million in 2030. Amounts are relatively modest, so it is helpful to keep in mind these are in millions of dollars rather than billions.

Using these projections in a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $7.49 per share. Against a recent share price of $7.50, the model suggests Uniti Group is around 0.2% overvalued, which is effectively in line with its DCF estimate.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Uniti Group is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Uniti Group.

Approach 2: Uniti Group Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually what matters most over time.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how investors see growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually justifies a lower one.

Uniti Group currently trades on a P/E of 1.13x. That sits well below the Telecom industry average of 16.44x and below the peer average of 10.70x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Uniti Group is 3.14x, which is its proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be, given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific drivers rather than assuming all Telecom stocks deserve similar multiples. With Uniti Group’s actual P/E of 1.13x versus a Fair Ratio of 3.14x, the shares screen as trading below that Fair Ratio estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

At A$8.88 per share, IGO is trading on a P/S of 12.7x. Our SWS DCF model points to a fair value of A$11.12, suggesting the share price and sales-based valuation are sending mixed signals.

The P/S multiple compares the company’s market value to its revenue and is often used where earnings are negative, as is the case for IGO. For a battery minerals business with unprofitable operations and an annual revenue of A$527.8m, a 12.7x P/S ratio implies the market is putting a high value on each dollar of current sales.

According to the fair ratio work, that 12.7x P/S sits far above an estimated fair P/S of 0.2x, a level the market could move closer to if sentiment cools. When you line IGO up against the wider Australian Metals and Mining industry, its 12.7x P/S is far lower than an industry average P/S of 143.2x.
